title: Fiber-integrated Quantum Frequency Conversion for Long-distance Quantum Networking
authors: Zhichuan Liao, Ao Shen, Lai Zhou, Nan Jiang, Zhiliang Yuan
abstract: Signal photons emitted by quantum nodes typically fall outside the low-loss telecom window of optical fibers, leading to severe transmission losses. Quantum frequency conversion (QFC) offers an effective optical interface that bridges quantum nodes with telecom-band channels, enabling long-distance quantum communication. In this work, we demonstrate a compact, fiber-integrated QFC system with low noise and a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Using a periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide, input photons at 637.2 nm are down-converted to telecom photons at 1588.3 nm. Our system achieves a total conversion efficiency of approximately 9%, with pump-induced noise suppressed to 154 Hz. For input photon rates of 32.7, 118.0, and 327.7 kHz, the corresponding SNRs are 12.3, 43.9, and 117.8, respectively. We further develop a theoretical model to simulate the entanglement fidelity between nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center spins and the frequency-converted telecom photons. At the emission rate of an NV center, our QFC system maintains an expected fidelity exceeding 52% over a transmission distance of 100 km. These findings highlight the potential of our QFC system for scalable, long-distance quantum networking.
summary: This paper presents a fiber-integrated quantum frequency conversion system designed to bridge the gap between quantum nodes and telecom-band channels. By using a PPLN waveguide and a multi-stage filtering system, the authors achieved high-efficiency conversion with very low noise. They demonstrated that this setup can maintain high entanglement fidelity over 100 km of fiber. This advancement is crucial for building scalable, long-distance quantum communication networks.
main_problem: Signal photons from quantum nodes like NV centers often emit at wavelengths incompatible with low-loss telecom fibers, causing high transmission loss in long-distance networks.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a fiber-integrated quantum frequency conversion system with 9% efficiency and extremely low noise (154 Hz), achieving an expected entanglement fidelity exceeding 52% over 100 km.
method: The researchers used a PPLN waveguide for down-conversion, implemented a multi-stage filtering module (DWDM, FBG, and UNTF) for noise suppression, and developed a theoretical model to simulate spin-photon entanglement fidelity.
model_or_system: The experimental platform is a fiber-integrated system using a periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide to convert 637.2 nm photons to 1588.3 nm. The quantum node model involves nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center electron spins using time-bin encoding.
key_observables: Conversion efficiency, pump-induced noise rate, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and entanglement fidelity.
important_parameters: Conversion efficiency of ~9%, noise suppression of >97 dB, 100 km transmission distance, and input photon rates ranging from 32.7 kHz to 327.7 kHz.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the NV center electron spin is initially prepared in a specific state and uses a specific sequence for generating time-bin entanglement; filtering performance is limited by the trade-off between noise suppression and insertion loss.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup; Fig 2 plots conversion efficiency and noise rate vs pump power; Fig 3 shows SNR vs pump power for different input rates; Fig 4 shows simulated entanglement fidelity vs fiber length.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of long-distance networking, describes the experimental setup and filtering architecture, presents experimental results for efficiency and noise, provides a theoretical model for fidelity, and concludes with the system's potential for scalability.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it addresses the practical engineering challenges of frequency conversion, noise mitigation, and maintaining entanglement over long-distance fiber links.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Experimental setup. A continuous-wave (CW) laser at 637.2 nm (signal laser) is carved into optical pulses using an acousto- optic modulator (AOM), and then attenuated to single photon level by two cascaded polarization maintaining manual variable optical attenuators (PMVOAs). The attenuated signal pulses are subsequently combined with a 1064.1 nm pump laser via dense wavelength division multiplexing (DWDM), then coupled into a periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide for fre- quency conversion. The converted photons are spectrally filtered using a combination of DWDM, fiber Bragg gratings (FBG), and a ultra-narrowband tunable optical filter (UNTF). A clock source, time...

Fig. 2. Conversion efficiency and noise versus pump power. The conversion efficiency for pulsed (circles) and CW (yellow diamonds) signal light is plotted as a function of pump power (left axis). The black solid line denotes a fit to Eq. (1). The cor- responding noise count rate is shown on the right axis (black squares).

Fig. 3. Signal-to-noise ratio of the converted signal as a func- tion of pump power. The circles represent the measured SNRs for input photon count rates of 32.7 kHz (red), 118.0 kHz (blue), and 327.7 kHz (green) at 637 nm.

Fig. 4. Simulated entanglement fidelity as a function of fiber length. The curves are obtained from a theoretical model under two parameter sets: our experimental results (solid lines) and those from Ref. [34] (dashed lines). The circle and diamond are calculated using the measured SNRs. The red solid line and circle correspond to a photon count rate of RS = 32.7 kHz (our work), and the red dashed line and di- amond correspond to RS = 35.5 kHz (Ref. [34]). The blue and green curves represent RS = 118.0 kHz and RS = 327.7 kHz, respectively. The circle marks an equivalent fiber length of 7.5 km, corresponding to 1.2 dB transmission loss between the two laboratories.
